nativeRange.habitat: Slow to moderately flowing rivers, streams, and flooded areas with dense riparian vegetation; clear to slightly turbid water over sandy or muddy substrates

decorPreferences: planted areas with fine-leaved plants (hornwort, water sprite, java moss) for cover
open central swimming space for schooling
dim or filtered lighting with floating plants
dark substrate to enhance colour contrast
driftwood and leaf litter for natural biotope feel

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, small flake food, and freeze-dried or frozen foods including brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, insects, worms, and plant material. Variety improves coloration and condition.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (rummy nose, ember tetra, neon tetra)
avoidWith: long-finned fish (angelfish, bettas, fancy guppies) — fin-nipping risk in groups under 8
aggressive or boisterous species that outcompete at feeding
very small nano fish or shrimp fry that could be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Aphyocharax anisitsi; typical mid-water school shot showing red fin coloration.

summary: The Bloodfin Tetra is a hardy, schooling characin from the Paraná River basin known for its striking red-tipped fins contrasting with a silvery-blue body. Unusually cool-water tolerant (18–28°C), it thrives in unheated tanks and is exceptionally long-lived for a tetra at 5–7 years.
careNotes: The Bloodfin Tetra (Aphyocharax anisitsi) is native to the Paraná River drainage of Argentina and Paraguay, where water temperatures can drop to 18°C in winter — making it one of the few tetras genuinely suited to unheated or minimally heated aquariums in temperate climates. A minimum tank of 60 L with at least 60 cm length is recommended for a school of 8+; they are active midwater swimmers that need horizontal swimming space. Water chemistry is forgiving across a wide range (pH 6.0–7.8, gH 2–20), reflecting their adaptability to varying conditions in the wild Paraná floodplain. Maintain groups of at least 8 to suppress mild fin-nipping tendency; smaller groups (under 6) can result in harassment of slower, long-finned tankmates. The species is easily confused with the True Bloodfin (Aphyocharax rathbuni), which displays more extensive red colouring covering much of the body rather than being restricted to the fins. A. anisitsi is significantly hardier and more widely available in the trade. This tetra is an excellent choice for beginners due to its robust constitution, tolerance of a wide temperature range, and long lifespan of 5–7 years — unusually long for a small characid. Provide varied feeding with quality flake, micro-pellets, and occasional frozen foods to maintain vibrant fin coloration.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that will spawn among fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop. A well-conditioned pair or small group can be triggered by slightly cooler, soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–5, temperature 22–24°C) and live or frozen foods. Adults readily eat their own eggs; remove parents or use a breeding net after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming within 4–5 days and accept infusoria, egg yolk, and commercial fry foods. Breeding in captivity is moderately easy with appropriate setup.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyocharax anisitsi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Paraná River basin, Argentina/Paraguay), water parameters (temperature 18–28°C, pH 6.0–7.8, hardness 36–357 ppm), adult size (5 cm), temperament, schooling requirements, diet, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyocharax anisitsi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (to 5.5 cm TL), native range (Paraná drainage, Argentina/Paraguay/Brazil), temperature range, and lifespan data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bloodfin Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide confirming beginner suitability, minimum school size of 6–8, unheated tank compatibility, and recommended tank size of 15+ gallons (57 L) for a school.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aphyocharax anisitsi species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed cool-water tolerance and distinction from A. rathbuni (True Bloodfin); noted fin-nipping tendency in small groups and long captive lifespan of 5–7 years.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Bloodfin Tetras in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Historical trade context; confirmed wide availability, hardiness, and notes on distinguishing A. anisitsi from A. rathbuni by extent of red colouration.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers; spawn among fine-leaved plants or spawning mop in soft, slightly acidic water. Adults eat eggs — remove after spawning. Fry hatch in 24–36 hours and accept infusoria initially.
